mapping.a_element: Fisher information associated with the distribution of momenta as a measure of thermodynamic temperature
mapping.b_element: analytical tools rooted in statistical physics used to derive error exponents

mapping.a_element: general probability density function for a physical system
mapping.b_element: finite-alphabet interference channel with codewords (non-i.i.d. process)

mapping.a_element: thermodynamic temperature as a derived quantity from information-theoretic properties
mapping.b_element: error exponents as derived quantities from statistical physics techniques

brief_justification: While both papers invoke statistical physics concepts, paper A defines a thermodynamic quantity (temperature) via Fisher information for a general distribution, whereas paper B uses statistical physics tools to analyze error exponents in a communication channel. The core mechanisms differ: A focuses on information-theoretic definitions of temperature, while B applies statistical physics to derive performance bounds for decoding, with no direct role-for-role correspondence in their underlying mathematics.
